coredata.dc:title: Modelling the electrical conductivity of Lithium-ion battery electrodes via a bonded-particle approach
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Society of Powder Technology Japan. Published by Elsevier B.V. and The Society of Powder Technology Japan.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Society of Powder Technology Japan. Published by Elsevier B.V. and The Society of Powder Technology Japan.
coredata.dc:creator: Sangrós Giménez, Clara
Pistoor, Astrid
Willuhn, Caroline
Schilde, Carsten
Kwade, Arno
coredata.dc:description: An electrode’s electrical conductivity greatly affects battery performance, justifying the need to fully understand the relation between particle microstructure and resulting electrical conductivity. This work proposes a model to assess the specific electrical conductivity of DEM electrode structures by analysing a network of conductive paths. NMC622- and NCA-based cathode structures, with a bond network representing the additive-binder matrix, are created and calendered numerically before being evaluated for electronic conductivity. The direct and bond contacts and, unprecedentedly, the internal particle resistances are taken into account to build a resistor network. The results obtained by this model are in great agreement with experimental electrical conductivities for NMC622- and NCA-based cathodes. Both the experimental cathodes and the developed conductivity model show an initial increase and later decrease of electrical conductivity as a function of calendering load, which is explained by a loss in particle bonds. Variations in the active material size distribution lead to the same changes in the electronic conductivity of experimental and simulated cathodes. This is attributed to the coordination number of the active material particles and the different types of particle contacts. To conclude, this model allows for a DEM simulation-independent, accurate evaluation of the electrical conductivity of any discrete particulate structure.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Battery
Electrode
DEM
Electrical conductivity
Resistor network

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Mechanical and physical parameters of active material particles
Specific electrical conductivity of active material powder
Specific electrical conductivity of LIB electrodes
Numerical model to determine the specific electrical conductivity

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the electrical resistances within the particulate and bond network regarded in the electrical model. Corresponding equations for the calculation of the electrical resistances
(a) Exemplary arrangement of connected particles with the corresponding resistances; (b) Analogous electrical circuit; (c) Final equivalent circuit to build the resistor network.
Step-by-step numerical procedure to model the electrical conductivity of a particulate network
Experimental set-up to measure the powder electrical conductivity (left); Analogous DEM simulation set-up (right)

Main structural parameters for the DEM simulations: size of the RVE (xsim, ysim, zsim), representative AM particle sizes and their corresponding quantities, and DEM snapshot of the electrode initial structures. The colours account for the particle sizes; red for the coarsest and blue for the smallest.
Experimental and modelled specific electrical conductivity for the non-calendered and calendered NMC622 cathodes
Experimental and modelled specific electrical conductivity for the non-calendered and calendered NMC622 cathodes

Average coordination number (
C N
¯
) and average number of bond connections per particle (
B
p
¯
) for the non-calendered and calendered NMC622 cathodes
Empirical and model specific electrical conductivity for the NCA-based cathodes. Empirical values are derived from the calculated function of Meyer [30]

Average coordination number (
C N
¯
) and average number of bond connections per particle (
B
p
¯
) for the simulated NCA-based cathodes
Experimental bulk electrical conductivity of NMC622 and NCA powders for four different packing densities
Calibrated values of the intrinsic resistivity of the AM particles and the intrinsic resistivity of a contact for the subsequent simulation of NMC622 and NCA electrodes
Required mechanical and physical parameters for the DEM characterization of active material particles

Main experimental properties of the electrodes used for the calibration and validation of the simulation work. (AM = active material, CC = conductive carbon, CG=conductive graphite, B = binder). a details on their manufacture addressed in [28];b details on their manufacture addressed in [30].

head.ce:title: Modelling the electrical conductivity of Lithium-ion battery electrodes via a bonded-particle approach

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Particle Technology, Technische Universität Braunschweig, Volkmaroder Str. 5, 38104 Braunschweig, Germany
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute for Particle Technology, Technische Universität Braunschweig, Volkmaroder Str. 5, 38104 Braunschweig, Germany

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An electrode’s electrical conductivity greatly affects battery performance, justifying the need to fully understand the relation between particle microstructure and resulting electrical conductivity. This work proposes a model to assess the specific electrical conductivity of DEM electrode structures by analysing a network of conductive paths.

NMC622- and NCA-based cathode structures, with a bond network representing the additive-binder matrix, are created and calendered numerically before being evaluated for electronic conductivity. The direct and bond contacts and, unprecedentedly, the internal particle resistances are taken into account to build a resistor network. The results obtained by this model are in great agreement with experimental electrical conductivities for NMC622- and NCA-based cathodes. Both the experimental cathodes and the developed conductivity model show an initial increase and later decrease of electrical conductivity as a function of calendering load, which is explained by a loss in particle bonds. Variations in the active material size distribution lead to the same changes in the electronic conductivity of experimental and simulated cathodes. This is attributed to the coordination number of the active material particles and the different types of particle contacts.
To conclude, this model allows for a DEM simulation-independent, accurate evaluation of the electrical conductivity of any discrete particulate structure.

ce:section.ce:para: Modelling lithium-ion batteries (LIB) offers the advantage of evaluating a wide range of parameters by saving material and energy resources associated with experimental tests. In the past years, various numerical approaches have been developed in order to study the operation of LIBs based on different criteria and specific purposes [1]. Besides, model-based and simulation studies provide the possibility to gather more information about the micro- and mesoscale. Within this context, the most commonly used battery cell models are the physics-based electrochemical models [2], equivalent circuit models to predict cell performance [3], atomistic models which may assist in developing new materials [4] and molecular dynamics models to study electrolyte/electrode interfaces [5]. In the last years, the discrete element method (DEM) has also been proved to offer an alternative to study LIB electrodes by considering the behaviour of active material (AM) particles and still enabling to derive electrode-related properties [6–9]. The electrode physical and chemical characteristics strongly affect the gravimetric and volumetric power and energy of the cells. Therefore, it is crucial to understand these properties in order to improve cell performance.

The electrochemical reaction taking place within the electrode while the LIB charges or discharges comprises two transport mechanisms: electron and ion conduction. As a result, both cathode and anode must present proper ionic and electrical conductivities to assure battery performance. A complete review of these conduction phenomena can be found in [10], including different theoretical, experimental and simulation approaches. Regarding ionic conduction, not only the motion of Li-ions through the electrolyte from the cathode to the anode and vice versa is important, but also the solid-state diffusion within the active material particles and between them. In the case of electrical conduction, the ability of the particulate coating to facilitate the transport of electrons depends on the materials and also the formulation that gives rise to a specific electrode structure. Due to the fact that the active materials used in the production of positive electrodes mainly have a very low electrical conductivity, conductive agents are added to the recipe. Some examples are carbon black or graphite, which can be found mainly embedded in the binder matrix. However, the amount of inert materials needs to be reduced to ensure a proper energy density of the LIB. As a result, the appropriate distribution and additive content are two imperative aspects to ensure an efficient electrical network without compromising the battery performance.

In terms of studying the electrical transport, several numerical methods have been developed as non-invasive alternatives in addition to experiments. Commonly, a continuum medium approach has been followed, which mainly involves the homogenization of the LIB structure [11,12]. As a result, it is not required to characterize the microstructure thoroughly, which can accelerate the numerical process. However, it excludes the possibility to study relationships between the microstructure and the electrical network. Despite this fact, the well-known Bruggemańs effective medium theory has achieved quantitatively good outcomes in the past [13]. In order to consider the electrode microstructure, a random resistor network approach has also been adopted with special focus on solid oxide fuel cell electrodes [14,15]. Moreover, the percolation theory, which is widely used to describe disordered systems, has also found its application within the context of LIB electrodes [16–18]. The percolation theory suggests the calculation of the electrical conductivity by taking the percolation probability into consideration; i.e., the probability that a conductive path can be found within the structure [19,20]. A critical additive concentration, also known as percolation threshold, can be defined based on the analysis of the coordination number of the particles. Over the past years, several percolation models for the determination of the percolation probability have been developed and applied to composite electrodes [18,21–24]. Despite its vast application, there are some aspects that are not reckoned when applying this theory. For instance, the presence of different materials with diverse electrical conductivities is only rarely considered. In addition, the directionality of the contacts and their quality (contact area) are not regarded, which may alter the outcome of the model to a great extent.

Besides the mentioned continuum model, random resistor network model, and percolation theory, several other models exist to describe electrical conductivity. The mean-field approach is a computationally efficient method for modelling electrical conductivity [25] but becomes impractical when moving from the nanoscale to the microscale, which is the scale of interest in this study. The fibre contact model has been successfully employed to model the electrical conductivity of carbon-polymer blends [26], focusing largely on carbon-fibre orientation and its impact on the percolation threshold. Percolation was also examined using a power law model in 2D space [27], which would result in very high computational costs for 3D space. In this study, the goal was to implement a model to evaluate the electrical conductivity of LIB electrodes by taking into consideration their particulate structure. By generating these structures firstly via DEM simulations, this method considers all active material particle contacts, internal particle resistances and the presence of additive-binder matrix. Moreover, the model was calibrated with the help of experimental analysis, which allows to determine the material electrical conductivities and integrate these in the establishment of the numerical electrical network. Ultimately, the specific electrical conductivity of real LIB electrodes was numerically derived using this model and correspondingly validated via experiments. As a result, this method may help to understand in detail the correlations between the structure and the electrical network with the view of improving electrode design.

The validity of the presented electrical model was proved for NMC622 (LiNi0.6Mn0.2Co0.2O2) and NCA (LiNi0.8Co0.15Al0.05O2) cathodes. In particular, a series of calendered NMC622-based electrodes with different porosities was studied along NCA-based electrodes consisting of different particle size distributions. Since the goal of this study was not to focus on the manufacturing of LIB electrodes but to implement a numerical electrical model, previous experimental work was considered. The non-calendered NMC622 electrode was produced and described within the work of Mayer et. al. [28]. The calendering experiments and the corresponding calendered electrodes were fulfilled and evaluated within the framework of the doctoral thesis of Sangrós [29]. The series of NCA electrodes were manufactured within the work of Meyer [30]. In order to produce them, Meyer made use of two NCA powders with two different particle sizes, namely one powder with a
d 50
of approximately 15 µm and another powder with a
d 50
of almost 6 µm. Both NCA powders were mixed in different mass proportions providing five samples which were used to produce the electrodes. The electrode containing only the 15-µm AM particles will be named NCA_100/0, while the electrode containing only the 6-µm AM particles will be named NCA_0/100. Furthermore, an electrode with 50wt.-% of both powders (NCA_50/50), one with 80wt.-% of 15-µm NCA and 20wt.-% of 6-µm NCA (NCA_80/20) and a cathode composed of 20wt.-% of 15-µm NCA and 80 wt.-% of 6-µm NCA (NCA_20/80) were manufactured. All five electrodes were calendered to reach a porosity of 0.3. Information regarding the electrode production and further specifications concerning the calendering process can be accessed in the corresponding publications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical and physical parameters of active material particles
ce:section.ce:para: Since the DEM is a numerical method that derives the behaviour of a granular material by analysing the response of its individual particles, it is of great importance to represent them correctly in the simulation setup (e.g., material properties, shape, size, initial velocity…). For this reason, single NCA and NMC622 particles were analysed experimentally. Firstly, laser diffraction particle size analyses were performed using the device Helos (Sympatec). Details about the measurement principle and the preparation of the samples can be found in [31]. Besides, microcompression experiments on single active material particles were carried out to establish the proper contact model and obtain important mechanical properties, namely the Younǵs modulus, the yield ratio, the coefficient of restitution and the particle strength. Further information about these parameters and how to obtain them are fully reported in [29]. In this regard, the TriboIndenter TI 900 indenter device from Hysitron (Inc. Minneapolis) was used. Specifics regarding the measurement procedure and the chosen parameters are adequately described in the work of Schilde et al. [32] and Sangrós et al. [7]. Lastly, the density of the raw powder materials was evaluated with a helium pycnometer ULTRAPYC 1200e from the company Quantachrome GmbH & Co. (Odelzhausen, Germany).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specific electrical conductivity of active material powder
ce:section.ce:para:
As it is fully explained in [33], 3 g of active material (AM) powder were firstly introduced into the insulated cylinder (cross-sectional area,
A
c y l
=1.13 cm2). Subsequently, an electrical current of 1 mA was applied. An external mechanical stress of 3.5GPa was provided to compact the sample while at the same time the electrical voltage, i.e. the electrical resistance of the powder, was measured. By knowing the packing height,
h
A M
p o w d e r
,
and the resulted electrical resistance,
R
A M
p o w d e r
, the specific electrical bulk conductivity
κ
e l ,
A M
p o w d e r
was calculated as:
(1)
κ
e l ,
A M
p o w d e r
=
h
A M
p o w d e r
R
A M
p o w d e r
·
A
c y l
The powder packing density,
ρ
A M
p o w d e r
, was reckoned from the volume of the filled cylinder and the total mass of the sample:
(2)
ρ
A M
p o w d e r
=
m
A M
p o w d e r
h
A M
p o w d e r
·
A
c y l

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specific electrical conductivity of LIB electrodes
ce:section.ce:para:
The specific electrical conductivity of the electrodes was determined using the Z020 materials testing machine from Zwick GmbH & Co. By applying the method proposed by Westphal et al. [34], the specific electrical conductivity of a circular sample of the electrodes was assessed (cross-sectional area
A
= 1.13 cm2) under an applied current of 10 mA and a contact normal stress from the equipment stamp of 350kPa. The compression of the electrode to a limited extent is required in order to homogenise the contact between the coating and the equipment stamp. The compaction velocity was set to 0.75 mm min-1 and the measuring time was 3 s. The electrical resistance
R
of five samples for each electrode was measured in order to assure representative values. From the measured electrical resistances, the bulk specific conductivity of the electrode coating can be calculated by taking its thickness and the size of the electrode sample into consideration.
(2)
κ
e l
=
h
c o a t i n g
R · A
The coating thickness is considered under the assumption that the current collector does not contribute to an increase in the electrical resistance of the measurement due to its excellent electrical conductivity properties. In fact, aluminium presents an electrical conductivity of approximately 3.45 · 107 S m-1 [35].

The first step for the modelling of the electrical conduction is to numerically generate the particulate electrode structure. For this purpose, the discrete element method (DEM) was chosen since the structural information that is required for the subsequent modelling is easy to access via this simulation method. The open source DEM simulation software LIGGGHTS was used as it has already been successfully applied in previous studies to analyse the structure and the mechanical behaviour of LIB electrodes [6–9,36]. The numerical generation of the electrode is performed based on several input information that can be obtained experimentally: mechanical properties of AM particles, AM particle size distribution, composition of the electrode (additive-binder matrix and AM relation) and electrode thickness and porosity. Correct representation of particle shape and particle size distribution is essential to achieve accurate conductivity results. Both aspects were found to predominantly influence the particle coordination number [37–39] and percolation threshold [40,41], which in turn directly affect electrical conductivity. Previous studies [6,7] provide SEM images of the herein used AM particles and show that these are nearly spherical, so spheres are chosen to represent the AM in the simulation. The same procedure adopted in the mentioned publications is followed in this work. The first step in the simulation is to choose the shape and size of the representative volume element (RVE). Within this work, the RVE was a rectangular prism with periodic boundaries in the transversal directions (
x
and
y
) and fixed walls in the axial direction (
z
). The height of the RVE (
z
s i m
) corresponds to the electrode thickness so that this parameter is settled a priori in the simulation. Once the total volume is acknowledged,
V
R V E
, the number of AM particles to fill the simulation box has to be calculated so that the porosity of the simulated RVE matches the porosity of the real LIB non-calendered electrode. The procedure of how LIGGGHTS positions the particles within the RVE and determines the amount of inserted particles is fully described in [42]. The sizes of the particles are chosen taking the experimental laser diffraction analysis of AM particles into account. Five representative AM particle sizes are chosen from the experimental size distribution measurement along with their frequency distribution. After the particles are located in the RVE, the next step is to account for the additive-binder matrix to create the bonds between AM particles. All features of the bond model and its implementation and application in the DEM environment are fully described in our previous work [6]. Since all electrodes evaluated in this study were calendered after the drying process, the simulation of the calendering step has to be accomplished by compacting the numerical structure. In order to do so, the methodology introduced by Sangrós et. al. [7] is followed till the aimed porosity is achieved.

Once the electrode structure is generated and the calendering step is simulated, the electrical modelling can begin based on the microstructure and the material properties. This is accomplished by a post-processing tool written in Python programming language that gathers the required information and uses it to create an electrical network. The implementation of the electrical model is fully described in the following Section.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Numerical model to determine the specific electrical conductivity
ce:section.ce:para:
Three files are required as inputs for the execution of the electrical model, which are generated by the DEM simulation:
•
The dump file: It contains the information regarding the ID, position within the RVE and radius of all AM particles.
•
The bond file: It consists of a list of all existing bonds in the simulation. As mentioned, bonds replace the additive-binder matrix in the simulation, bringing cohesion between AM particles. This file includes the ID pairs of bonded particles which, in combination with the first dump file, allows the determination of the length and cross-sectional area of all bonds.
•
The direct contact file: It covers the information related to all direct particle contacts, i.e., the ID pairs of particles directly in contact and the contact area.

By combining these three files, the electrical network can be fulfilled for a simulated particulate structure. In the following lines, the calculation of the electrical resistances is explained firstly and subsequently, the description of the numerical model is provided.

In order to reckon every electrical resistance, the program labels all contacts between AM particles as bond or direct contacts. In the case two particles are bonded and also in direct contact, it is noted as well. Once the geometrical information is gathered from the three input files (dump, bond and direct contact files), the electrical resistances can be calculated. In the literature, the random resistance network approach has been already adopted to evaluate the thermal [43], electrical [16] and ionic conduction [15] within particulate electrodes. The main difference between these models and the one proposed in this work lies on the calculation of the individual resistances. The three-dimensional model of Ott et al. [16] considered the contact between particles to be ideal, as well as the model of Abbaspour et al. [15]. On the contrary, four different cases are distinguished within this present study and hence, four different resistances. They are summarized in Fig. 1 and explained in the next lines.

The first case is the particle inner resistance
R
i , j k
p
, i.e., the internal resistance an AM particle exhibits to the flow of electrons. Since it is material-dependent, it is calculated taking the intrinsic resistivity of the material into account,
ϱ
p
e l
:
(4)
R
i , j k
p
=
L
i j - i k
π 2
(
r
c , i k
2
+
r
b , i j
2
)
ϱ
p
e l
In the literature, some models consider the particle diameter as the parameter to be proportional to the inner resistance [15]. However, in this work, the internal resistance is considered to be directly proportional to the distance between consecutive direct or bond contacts,
L
i j - i k
. The reason is that the electron transport occurs from contact to contact to fulfil the path between the current collector and the separator without necessarily passing through the centre of the particle. Likewise,
R
i , j k
p
is inversely proportional to the arithmetic mean of the consecutive direct or bond contact areas (either
π
r
c , i j
2
or
π
r
b , i j
2
) that virtually form a conical frustum along the AM particle. The calculation of these contact areas is explained below.

Fig. 2 a depicts the internal resistances calculated for particle
2
, which is in contact with particles
1
,
3
and
4
. If electrons shall flow from particle
1
to particle
3
through particle
2
, the path designated as
R
2 , 13
p
in the mentioned figure shall be preferred since it is the shortest and most efficient. The cross-sectional area was calculated taking the average value of the contact areas of consecutive direct or bond contacts. As a result, if an AM particle is in contact with two other AM particles (particle
1
in Fig. 2a), one single internal resistance is calculated. For higher number of contacts per AM particle (particle
2
in Fig. 2a), the amount of internal resistances increases as the combination of possible electron paths increments.

The contact resistances can be divided in three cases depending on the type of contact. The resistance which arises when two AM particles are in contact but not bonded,
R
i j
c
, is reckoned taking the area and the intrinsic resistivity of the contact into consideration,
π
r
c , i j
2
and
ζ
c
e l
, respectively:
(5)
R
i j
c
=
1
π
r
c , i j
2
ζ
c
e l
The contact radius,
r
c , i j
, corresponds to the radius of the plane formed at the intersection between the overlapping particles and its calculation is performed based on the geometrical equations of both spherical particles:
(6)
r
c , i j
=
1
2
L
i j
4
L
i j
2
r
i
2
-
(
L
i j
2
-
r
j
2
+
r
i
2
)
The determination of
ζ
c
e l
is not a straightforward task. In this work, this was performed with the help of experimental conductivity measurements of AM powder, explained in detail in the next Section. The mechanical and also the electrical performance of the additive-binder matrix is considered as a unity since the small additive particles are embedded in the polymeric binder. This approach has already been taken by other authors [44,45] and the presented method regards it through the bond model. The additive-binder matrix has a higher electrical conductivity than the AM particles, hence, the resistance of a bond is lower than that of a direct contact. Chen et al. [46] already studied the electrical conductivity of different combinations of polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) and carbon black (CB), which are also the materials considered in this work. The intrinsic resistivity of the bonds,
ϱ
b
e l
, was taken from their experimental work regarding the PVDF/CB weight ratio presented in the electrode formulations. The electrical resistance of a bond,
R
i j
b
, is proportional to the length of the bond, determined from the distance between the particle centres and their radii. It is also inversely proportional to the cross-sectional area of the bond,
π
r
b , i j
2
, considering that the bond radius is the radius of the smallest particle involved in the bond.
(7)
R
i j
b
=
L
i j
-
(
r i
+
r j
)
π
r
b , i j
2
ϱ
b
e l
Lastly, the resistance taking place when two AM particles are direct in contact and bonded,
R
i j
b c
, is also regarded, which has to be considerably lower than the inner resistance of AM particles and the contact resistance due to the presence of a bond (additive-binder matrix). Because of the additional high proximity of the particles,
R
i j
b c
is considered to be inversely proportional to the bond area and directly proportional to the resistance of the contact,
ζ
c
e l
:
(8)
R
i j
b c
=
1
π
r
b , i j
2
ζ
c
e l
Once all inner particle resistances as well as bond and direct contact resistances are calculated, the network is built by defining the nodes within the structure. This task is not straightforward since the centres of the particles do not constitute the nodes between resistances. This is due to the novel consideration of the inner particle resistance of this methodology, in comparison to [15] or [16]. For a better clarification, a simple example of four connected particles is shown in Fig. 2 together with the corresponding equivalent circuit. The identified nodes are
A
,
B
and
C
, which are not located in the centre of the particles but between particles contacts, as it can be discerned in the picture. The corresponding resistances of all branches are also presented. In the case three branches are met in a node, the value of the contact resistance (bond or direct contact) is divided by three and added equally to each branch. Taking a look at Fig. 2, this applies to the direct contact resistance
R
12
c
, for instance. The value of
R
12
c
is distributed around node
A
as displayed in the equivalent circuit (Fig. 2b). Since
R
12
c
/ 3
is now connected in series to
R
2 , 13
p
and
R
23
b
/ 3
, they can be summed and replaced by a one equivalent resistor,
R
A B
e q
(Fig. 2c). This is performed for all resistances located in series and in between same nodes. Breaking up the bond and contact resistances in the proposed way introduces a slight electronic inaccuracy, as electrons travelling in series and in parallel are treated equally. However, for proving the concept of using a python script that allows for immediate and easy analysis of numerical particle structures, we chose this method of resistance distribution. The simplicity of this script, the sufficient accuracy, visible when comparing simulated and experimental results, and the elimination of need for installing costly external software, which might not represent the structure accurately enough, greatly overweigh the simplification made to the resistor network.

Once all equivalent resistances are calculated, the numerical model starts analysing the network graph by performing a matrix representation, an approach which has already been taken for different machine learning applications [47]. The goal is to build a spanning tree out of the whole network of particle and bond connections. In order to explain the numerical procedure, a simple example is presented in Fig. 3 . Nodes between particles are marked (
A
,
B
and
C
) as well as the start (
S
) and target nodes (
T
), which close the electrical circuit. Likewise, the equivalent resistances are depicted representatively between the corresponding nodes.

After fulfilling step 1 (see Fig. 3), a list is compiled with the values of the equivalent resistances and the nodes they are connected to (see Fig. 3, step 2). Taking this information into account, the next stage consists of creating a symmetric matrix whose dimensions correspond to the number of defined nodes. The indices of the rows and columns are the arranged nodes and the elements of the matrix can be either zero (no connection between nodes) or one (nodes are connected). This matrix is created in order to accelerate the calculation time (see Fig. 3, step 3). In fact, the program examines only the elements above the main diagonal which are equal to one and generates a second symmetric matrix containing the values of the corresponding resistances between nodes, which are accessible from the list elaborated at the beginning (see Fig. 3, step 4). Subsequently, a Laplacian matrix (
L
) is created to represent the network graph. The non-diagonal elements of this matrix are the inverse of the elements of the second matrix (inverse of the equivalent resistance between nodes) whereas the diagonal is the sum of all elements in a row (see Fig. 3, step 5). The Laplacian matrix of a graph has the remarkable property that any of its (
i , j
)-minors are equal to the number of spanning trees of the corresponding graph [48], which is also known as the Kirchhoff́s matrix-tree theorem. Accordingly, the last row and column are removed and the resulting Laplacian matrix is multiplied by the voltage vector following Ohḿs law. The indices of the voltage vector correspond to the listed nodes and its components are unknown. The result of this operation is the electrical current vector, whose components are set to zero except for the start and target nodes, whose values equal to one. Consequently, the total voltage can be obtained by solving this linear system of equations (see Fig. 3, step 6). Finally, the total resistance
R
t o t
can be derived applying Ohḿs law to the whole system and considering the calculated voltage and the total current to be 1 A, as set previously (see Fig. 3, step 7). From the total electrode resistance, the specific electrical conductivity of the electrode,
κ
e l
, can be determined as:
(8)
R
t o t
=
U
t o t
I
t o t
→
κ
e l
=
1
R
t o t
In contrast to the effective medium theory or the percolation theory, this method enables to consider the microstructure of the electrode. The electrode porosity affects the quality of the contacts as well as the coordination number of the AM particles. By implication, the outcome of this method would also be influenced because a higher coordination number leads to a higher number of contact resistances. Likewise, at low porosities, the cross-sectional contact area between two particles increases, which would affect the calculation of the corresponding contact resistance. Moreover, the particle size distribution influences not only the inner particle resistances but also the contact areas. A higher electrode thickness implies an increment in the number of resistances in the direction perpendicular to the current collector, whereas changes in the electrode composition affect the number of bonds and, hence, the corresponding resistance calculations.

The intrinsic resistivity of the AM particles,
ϱ
p
e l
, as well as the contact resistance,
ζ
c
e l
,
have to be calibrated for the correct fulfilment of the electrical model. Since these two parameters are solely related to the material properties of the AM, it was decided to determine the bulk conductivity of the pure AM powder and from this measurement, to accomplish the calibration of both values. The bulk electrical conductivity was measured as indicated in the experimental section. Due to the fact that the conductivity of a particulate material is dependent on the number and quality of particle contacts, this experimental technique monitors the electrical conductivity during the compression of the sample. The packing density can be determined stepwise considering the amount of powder used to fill the die, the dimensions of the die and the height of the powder sample (Equation 2). As a result, the evolution of the bulk electrical conductivity can be determined as a function of the packing density of the powder.

The application of the electrical model was run for two electrode chemistries: NMC622 and NCA. Consequently, the electrical properties of both active materials had to be experimentally assessed. The procedure, which was the same for both materials, was subsequently reproduced via DEM simulations in order to accomplish the calibration of the mentioned parameters. In particular, a bed of the corresponding AM particles was created within a cubic simulation box of 150 µm length with periodic boundaries. After thorough investigation, such a RVE with 150 µm edge length was found to be the smallest simulation domain possible, before the electrode porosity would not be accurate anymore [29]. Due to the fact that the electrical conductivity values are height-specific, it is not imperative to represent the whole thickness of the die in the simulation. The particle size distribution as well as the initial porosity of the packing were the same as the ones obtained experimentally. Since the measurement of powder conductivity involves only AM material, no bonds were created in the simulation. By placing an imported STL-mesh, the particle bed was compressed taking the experimental compaction rate into consideration. Fig. 4 presents a schematic description of the experimental set-up and a snapshot of its representation via DEM. As in the measurement, the application of pressure increases the contact area between the AM particles in the simulation. Furthermore, elastic and plastic deformation of the particles takes place, which is also captured by the simulation.

Four packing densities with their corresponding specific electrical conductivity values were selected from the experimental data for both NMC622 and NCA (Table 1 ). These compaction stages were also simulated and the corresponding DEM structures were analysed in terms of their electrical conductivity by means of the electrical model. The purpose was to match the modelled global electrical conductivity with the experimental value by iteratively fitting the intrinsic resistivity of the AM particles,
ϱ
p
e l
, and the contact resistance,
ζ
c
e l
. The numerical process finished when the absolute difference between modelled and experimental bulk parameters was equal or lower than 5% of the experimental value. This condition had to be fulfilled for the four selected packing densities.

The final values for
ϱ
p
e l
and
ζ
c
e l
that will be used for the simulations are presented in Table 2 . It can be noted that the bulk electrical conductivity is lower than the intrinsic conductivity of an AM particle, calculated as the inverse of
ϱ
p
e l
. This is coherent with the fundamentals of electrical conduction within a particle network. The bulk electrical conductivity is lower than that of the individual particles due to the additional resistance offered by the contact resistance between particles, which hinders electron transport. Besides, it can be observed that the
ϱ
p
e l
of NMC622 particles is distinctly higher than that of NCA, which can be related to the amount of nickel. As already discussed in the literature [49,50], an increase in the nickel content implies an increment in nickel atoms transiting to Ni3+, which enhances the density of the electron carriers and hence, improves the electrical conductivity.

Firstly, the numerical structures of the initial non-calendered electrodes were generated via DEM. In order to do so, several particle properties were determined experimentally and introduced as input parameters in the simulation setup. The mechanical parameters of NCA and NMC622 particles are gathered in Table 3 along with the experimental values of
d 10
,
d 50
and
d 90
.

Additionally, the global porosity and thickness of the non-calendered structures were also considered in order to create the particulate coatings via DEM. These parameters along with the electrode composition are gathered in Table 4 . Once the electrodes were numerically generated, the calendering process was simulated with the aim of achieving the final porosity and corresponding thickness of the experimental electrodes (see Table 4). The full description of the methodology to create the initial electrodes and simulate the calendering step can be found in [7].
Fig. 5 gathers the size of the simulated RVE along with the particle sizes and number of particles simulated via DEM for each non-calendered electrode structure.

For structure comparison between numerically and experimentally produced cathodes SEM images of the cathodes can be found in Meyer et al. [30] and Sangrós et al. [7]. First, the images justify the choice of spherical particles for the DEM simulation. Second, since NCM particle cracking was only found for electrodes calendered to porosities of 25 % [53], which is lower than the here studied electrodes, and their reported cracking did not substantially influence the electrochemical performance, the possible but improbable particle cracking was neglected in this study.

The electrical model was proved for NMC622- and NCA-based cathodes. As described in the Material Section, a series of calendered NMC622-based electrodes with different porosities was studied along NCA-based electrodes consisting of different particle size distributions but same porosity. Thus, the impact of the calendering degree as well as the effect of the particle size distribution on the electrical conduction can be independently evaluated via this modelling tool. For the correct fulfilment of the electrical model, the calibrated values of
ϱ
p
e l
and
ζ
c
e l
presented in the previous Section (Table 2) were considered along with a
ϱ
b
e l
of 0.238 Ω cm. This last parameter was taken from the experimental work of Chen et. al [46] for a binder-additive ratio of 1:1.

All calibrated and literature-based values used in our resistor network were subjected to a sensitivity analysis to assess the criticality of the calibrated parameters and to identify the resistances that dominate the network. Fig. 6 displays the results and shows that the newly introduced inner particle resistance has a significant impact on the overall electronic conductivity. Even small variations in this parameter largely determine the electronic conductivity, which confirms the necessity of integrating it in the network model. Variations in contact and bond resistance has a smaller influence on the overall conductivity. We attribute the limited influence of contact resistivity to the fact that the contact areas are much smaller than the size of the active material particles, leading to a less relevant influence on the overall electronic conductivity. The small influence of bond resistivity is probably caused by the close proximity of the particles, resulting in short bond lengths and therefore negligible bond resistance. This graph shows that our addition of taking into account the internal particle resistances is relevant to calculate the overall electrical conductivity.

Fig. 7 displays the specific electrical conductivity of the non-calendered electrode (NMC622_NC) with a porosity of 0.43 and the calendered electrodes with lower porosities measured experimentally and determined via the modelling tool. Taking advantage of the simulated structures, the implemented tool was able to analyse the particle network and evaluate the electrical conduction by considering all structural and connectivity features. The modelled values could represent the experimental tendency to a good extent. As explained, the numerical model regards not only the AM particles but also the additive-binder matrix, which certainly plays a much more relevant role in the electrical conduction. Besides, it enables a full characterization of the evolution of the particulate network.

Fig. 8 shows the average coordination number (
C N
¯
), which is the average number of contact points each particle has with its neighbours, and the average number of bond connections per particle (
B
p
¯
) calculated as the average number of bond connections per particle for the non-calendered and for every calendered structure. Thus, insight into the mechanical integrity of the electrodes can be analysed. Overall, it can be seen that the
C N
¯
increases with an increment in the calendering line load, i.e., with a reduction in the final electrode porosity. Albeit most particles must be overlapping due to the applied calendering pressure, direct particle-particle contacts without bonds in between are scarce. This is because most particles are connected by bonds, which represent the carbon-binder-domain within the electrode. This is realistic, as the active material in the real electrode is always surrounded by the electrochemically inactive material. Regarding the bond connections, electrode NMC622_C3, with the lowest porosity, exhibits a lower
B
p
¯
in comparison to electrode NMC622_C2. This loss of bonds is a consequence of the high mechanical stress imposed on the structure, which leads to an intensive displacement of the AM particles and thus, a deterioration of the additive-binder matrix. Due to this fact and considering that the intrinsic resistivity of a bond is lower than that of a contact, the electrical model determines a lower electrical conductivity for electrode NMC622_C3, which agrees with the experimental trend (see Fig. 7). Furthermore, deviations between the model and the experiment can be well associated rather to the numerical structures than to the electrical model itself. For instance, the surface roughness of the AM particles and their mechanical damage in terms of particle fracture are not considered in the generation of the electrode structure. These aspects could be incorporated in the future without overlooking the additional computational time in order to enhance the model. Overall, the outcomes confirm the applicability of the numerical method and the calibration of its input-parameters for NMC622-based cathodes.

The NCA-based electrodes consisting of different particle size distributions were studied likewise. As mentioned, the experimental work of Meyer was considered for this purpose [30]. Meyer manufactured a series of NCA-based electrodes and calendered them under different calendering loads. Afterwards, the electrical conductivity of the samples was analysed experimentally and presented as an empirical function dependent on the final electrode porosity obtained after the calendering process. The calculated function achieved by Meyer and presented in his work [30] was regarded to obtain the empirical values of the specific electrical conductivity at a porosity of 0.3 and validate the numerical outcomes.

Fig. 9 displays the empirical as well as the modelled values of the specific electrical conductivity corresponding to the NCA-based electrodes. In view of the outcomes, it can be stated that the model proposed in this work can also be applied to analyse this cathode chemistry. In fact, the experimental trend could be reproduced via the generation of the DEM structures and further numerical analysis of the electron conduction. Like for the NMC-based electrodes, deviations between both values can be attributed to the numerically generated structures in terms of the ideal spherical particle form or the homogeneous distribution of the bonds. Thus, the influence of the AM particle size distribution on the electrical conductivity can be explained by taking a closer look at the connectivity of the corresponding structures, which is feasible by means of the numerical study. Besides, in light of the results, the definition of the different resistances according to Fig. 1 as well as the numerical procedure can be validated.

Fig. 10 presents the average coordination number (
C N
¯
) as well as the average number of bond connections per particle (
B
p
¯
) for every calendered NCA-based structure, which are decisive in order to comprehend the specific electrical conductivity. Firstly, it can be seen that the cathode NCA_80/20, which presents the lowest specific electrical conductivity of 0.17 S m-1, also displays the lowest values of
B
p
¯
and
C N
¯
, which are 3.24 and 0.31, respectively. Hence, it can be stated that the electron conduction within this cathode is inferior with regard to the other electrode structures because its particle assembly is significantly worse connected on account of its PSD. It is noteworthy that both NCA_100/0 and NCA_50/50 show similar values of
B
p
¯
and
C N
¯
, which is in accordance to their high values of specific electrical conductivity depicted in Fig. 7. Nevertheless, the specific electrical conductivity of NCA_50/50 is moderately lower, 0.29 S m-1 compared to 0.36 S m-1, which can be related to its slightly higher value of
C N
¯
. In order for a LIB electrode to have a proper electrical conductivity, the electron conduction from the substrate to the separator has to be assured. As a result, if the particle network is sufficiently connected in the direction perpendicular to the current collector, an increase in particle contacts in the direction parallel to the current collector would imply an increment in electrical resistances and, thus, a decrease in the electrical conductivity of the electrode. This fact also explains the difference between cathodes NCA_20/80 and NCA_0/100. Both structures exhibit a higher
B
p
¯
compared to the remaining electrodes, namely 3.77 and 3.72 in comparison to 3.39 for the NCA_50/50 electrode, and hence, electron conduction should be expected to be favoured. However, at the same time, their
C N
¯
of 1.15 and 1.25 is also higher than the
C N
¯
of 0.91 for NCA_50/50, leading to more conductive paths within the particulate structure and consequently, an increase in the number of electrical resistances. The overall ionic conductivity for NCA_20/80 and NCA_0/100 is therefore reduced by 25 % and 31 % compared to NCA_50/50. As a result, it can be derived from the numerical analysis that the PSD has a direct impact in the electrical conduction of an electrode and that a compromise between
B
p
¯
and
C N
¯
is essential to assure the sufficient connectivity between the AM particles. More specifically, the results suggest that one requirement for a structure of high electronic conductivity is a high
B
p
¯
, in our case above 3.37, meaning a homogeneous distribution of inactive material between the AM particles. This must be coupled with a moderate
C N
¯
, here just below 1.0, to achieve sufficient contacts for percolation but few enough to not increase contact resistances.

A model is presented to determine the specific electrical conductivity of LIB electrodes. By generating the particulate structures via discrete element method (DEM) and further simulating the calendering process, the specific electrical conductivity of NCA and NMC622 electrodes is modelled and validated with experimental results. The method proposes a novel approach to individually detect and calculate the possible resistances within the particulate structure. In fact, the model determines inner active material particle resistances as well as bond and direct particle-particle contact resistances. Subsequently, the numerical model builds a network from all particle connections and determines the specific electrical conductivity by also regarding the electrical and mechanical properties of the active material and the additive-binder matrix.

Great agreement could be reached between the modelled and the experimental electrical conductivity for NMC622- as well as for NCA-based cathodes. A potential advantage of this numerical method in contrast to the effective medium theory or the percolation theory is that the electrode microstructure is indirectly considered. Other studies that do consider the particulate microstructure also used resistor networks to model the granulate structure’s electronic conductivity [15,39,54,55]. In contrast to these publications, our proposed model specifically includes resistances of the inactive material between the AM particles and performs the simulations on a range of different realistic cathode microstructures developed by DEM simulation. Both novelties lead to more realistic results, as evidenced by the strong alignment of simulated and experimentally obtained electrical conductivities. In this framework, the experimental initial increase and posterior decrease of the electrical conductivity as a function of the calendering load could be reproduced by the model and explained by means of a loss in particle bonds. Likewise, a change in electron conduction due to a variation of the active material particle size distribution could be achieved by the numerical method and justified through a compromise between the coordination number of active material particles, the direct contact and bond area and the number of bond connections.

In view of its performance, it is believed that the implemented model can be transferred to other electrode chemistries with spherical AM particles such as anodes composed of round graphite or LiCoO2-based cathodes, which are also predominantly spherical. Likewise, other fields of applications such as the electronics industry could make profit of this numerical tool to investigate electrically-conductive particulate coatings.

Clara Sangrós Giménez: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Astrid Pistoor: Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Caroline Willuhn: Writing – review & editing. Carsten Schilde: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Arno Kwade: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support by the Federal Ministry for Economic Affairs and Energy (BMWi) within the research project ‘‘BaSiS – Batteriesicherheitssimulation”. Furthermore, we would like to express our sincere thanks to Chris Meyer and Julian Mayer for providing the experimental results.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fundamental challenges in packing problems: from spherical to non-spherical particles
